title: NA-ULIP
word_text:
Taxability of ULIP on surrender
Unit-linked insurance plans, or ULIPs as they are popularly called, provide life insurance coverage along with market-linked returns. You can choose a ULIP to make the future of your loved ones financially secured while also getting the opportunity to create a corpus  for your financial goals . These plans also offer liquidity during the policy tenure (post the lock in period) through their partial withdrawal feature. This feature allows you to withdraw up to a specified part of your fund value in case of exigencies. But what if you want to exit the policy completely? Is it possible?
It is, and it is called surrendering the ULIP, and it has tax implications too. Let’s understand the taxability of ULIPs on surrender. But first, the basics.
What Are ULIP Plans?
ULIPs are life insurance plans that combine the benefit of life insurance coverage and market-linked returns   in one plan. A part of the premium that you pay towards a ULIP is used to provide life insurance coverage. The other part is allocated to market-linked funds, which grow according to market performance.
ULIPs offer the flexibility of top-ups, switching, partial withdrawals, etc., that help you manage your investments the way you want to.
What Is the Meaning of Surrender in a ULIP Policy?
When you buy a ULIP, you choose a policy term. This term is the period over which the policy runs. However, if you want to exit the policy before the term is over, it is called a surrender. In other words, surrendering a unit-linked plan means exiting the policy before the term is over.
ULIP Surrender Taxation
When you surrender your ULIP, there might be specific tax implications depending on when you surrender the policy. ULIPS have a lock-in period of 5 years, and the tax implication depends on whether you are surrendering the policy during the lock-in period or after it.
Tax Rules Depending on Lock-In
Here’s a look at the rules of tax on ULIP surrender depending on when it is surrendered -
Surrender Before 5 Years
If you are surrendering the ULIP before 5 years, the tax benefits on ULIP premiums availed under the provisions of Section 80C (under old tax regime) of the Income Tax Act of 1961 would be reversed. The deduction availed in the previous years would be considered as income in the year of surrender. Moreover, you would not be allowed to get the 80C (under old tax regime)  on the premiums paid for the year in which you are surrendering the policy.
Surrender After 5 Years
If you surrender the policy after 5 years, the 80C benefit (under old tax regime) would not be reversed. Moreover, the surrender value that you receive would be tax-free in your hands under the provisions of Section 10(10D) if the premiums paid were up to 10% of the sum assured (for policies that have been issued on or after 1st April 2012) or 20% of the sum assured (for policies that were issued on or before 31st March 2012). Moreover, you can claim an exemption under the provisions of Section 10(10D) if the aggregate premiums paid are up to ₹2.5 lakhs for policies that have been issued on or after 1st February 2021. For policies that were issued before this date, there’s no maximum premium criterion, and the surrender value enjoys tax exemption if the premium is up to 10% or 20% of the sum assured, as the case may be.
How Policy Issue Date Affect Tax Rules?
As is evident from the previous section, the date when the policy was issued also affects the taxability of the surrender value. Here’s how -
ULIPs Issued Before 1st February 2021
If the ULIP was issued before 1st February 2021 and it is surrendered after the lock-in period of 5 years, the exemption under the provisions of Section 10(10D) would be available if the premium is up to 10% of the sum assured (20% for policies that were issued before 1st April 2012).
ULIPs Issued on 1st February 2021 or After this Date.
If you buy a ULIP anytime on or after 1st February 2021, the premium limit of ₹2.5 lakhs would apply. The surrender benefit would be tax-free only if the aggregate premium for all ULIPs is up to ₹2.5 lakhs and premium is up to 10% of the sum assured. If the premium is more than this amount, the Section 10(10D) exemption would not be allowed.
Key Takeaways
ULIPs are life insurance plans that provide life insurance protection along with the potential of market-linked returns.
If you exit the ULIP before the maturity date, it is called surrendering the policy.
You are eligible for a surrender value, which can be taxed depending on when you surrender the policy.
If you surrender the policy during the lock-in period of 5 years, the premium deduction allowed under the provisions of Section 80C in the previous years would be reversed.
If you surrender the ULIP after the lock-in period, the surrender value would be tax-free under the provisions of Section 10(10D) if the premium is up to 10% of the sum assured (for policies that have been issued on or after 1st April 2012) and the aggregate premium is up to ₹2.5 lakhs (for policies that were issued on or after 1st February 2021).
Conclusion
Surrendering a unit-linked insurance plan is not recommended, as you lose both the life insurance coverage and the potential for market-linked returns over the policy term. As such, it is feasible to continue ULIP till the chosen policy tenure. However, if you exit the ULIP prematurely and surrender it, you might earn a surrender value. Know the ULIP taxation on surrender, which depends on when you surrender the policy, the premium to sum assured ratio, and the date you bought the ULIP. Knowing the tax implications of surrender can help you make informed financial decisions that can minimise your tax liability. You can also use a ULIP calculator to estimate the fund value that you can get on surrender or maturity.
FAQs
What is the meaning of surrendering in a ULIP policy?
Surrendering in a ULIP plan means exiting the policy before the maturity date.
What will happen if I choose to surrender my ULIP policy?
If you surrender the ULIP, you would get a surrender value. However, the treatment of the ULIP after surrender depends on when you surrender. If you surrender the policy within the lock-in period, the fund value after deducting the applicable discontinuance charges, shall be credited to the discontinued policy fund and the risk cover shall cease. At the end of the lock-in period, the proceeds of the discontinuance fund shall be paid to the policyholder. But if you surrender the policy after the lock-in period , the fund value as on date of surrender will be paid , after deduction of applicable charges...
Is ULIP subject to taxation?
The surrender or maturity benefit received from a ULIP is allowed as an exemption under the provisions of Section 10(10D) if the premium is up to 10% of the sum assured (for policies that have been issued on or after 1st April 2012 and the aggregate premium is up to ₹2.5 lakhs for policies bought on or after 1st February 2021.
If the ULIPs were issued before 1st February 2021, would there be no surrender tax?
ULIPs issued before 1st February 2021 can get a tax-free surrender value only if they are surrendered after the lock-in period.
Disclaimer
IN THIS POLICY, THE INVESTMENT RISK IN INVESTMENT PORTFOLIO IS BORNE BY THE POLICYHOLDER
The Unit Linked Insurance Products do not offer any liquidity during the first five years of the contract. The policyholder will not be able to surrender or withdraw the monies invested in Unit Linked Insurance Products completely or partially till the end of the fifth year.
ULIPs are different from the traditional insurance products and are subject to the risk factors. The premium paid in ULIPs are subject to investment risks associated with capital markets and the NAVs of the units may go up or down based on the performance of fund and factors influencing the capital market and the insured is responsible for his/her decisions. Please know the associated risks and the applicable charges, from your Insurance agent or the Intermediary or policy document issued by the insurance company. The various funds offered under this contract are the names of the funds and do not in any way indicate the quality of these plans, their future prospects and returns.
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. For more details on risk factors, terms and conditions please read the sales brochure & policy document (available on www.bajajlifeinsurance.com) carefully before concluding a sale. Tax benefits as per prevailing Section 10(10D) and Section 80C (under old tax regime) of the Income Tax Act shall apply. You are requested to consult your tax consultant and obtain independent advice for eligibility before claiming any benefit under the policy

Taxability of ULIP on surrender
Unit-linked insurance plans, or ULIPs as they are popularly called, provide life insurance coverage along with market-linked returns. You can choose a ULIP to make the future of your loved ones financially secured while also getting the opportunity to create a corpus for your financial goals . These plans also offer liquidity during the policy tenure (post the lock in period) through their partial withdrawal feature. This feature allows you to withdraw up to a specified part of your fund value in case of exigencies. But what if you want to exit the policy completely? Is it possible?
It is, and it is called surrendering the ULIP, and it has tax implications too. Let’s understand the taxability of ULIPs on surrender. But first, the basics.
What Are ULIP Plans?
ULIPs are life insurance plans that combine the benefit of life insurance coverage and market-linked returns in one plan. A part of the premium that you pay towards a ULIP is used to provide life insurance coverage. The other part is allocated to market-linked funds, which grow according to market performance.
ULIPs offer the flexibility of top-ups, switching, partial withdrawals, etc., that help you manage your investments the way you want to.
What Is the Meaning of Surrender in a ULIP Policy?
When you buy a ULIP, you choose a policy term. This term is the period over which the policy runs. However, if you want to exit the policy before the term is over, it is called a surrender. In other words, surrendering a unit-linked plan means exiting the policy before the term is over.

When you surrender your ULIP, there might be specific tax implications depending on when you surrender the policy. ULIPS have a lock-in period of 5 years, and the tax implication depends on whether you are surrendering the policy during the lock-in period or after it.
Tax Rules Depending on Lock-In
Here’s a look at the rules of tax on ULIP surrender depending on when it is surrendered -
1. Surrender Before 5 Years
If you are surrendering the ULIP before 5 years, the tax benefits on ULIP premiums availed under the provisions of Section 80C (under old tax regime) of the Income Tax Act of 1961 would be reversed. The deduction availed in the previous years would be considered as income in the year of surrender. Moreover, you would not be allowed to get the 80C (under old tax regime) on the premiums paid for the year in which you are surrendering the policy. 2. Surrender After 5 Years
If you surrender the policy after 5 years, the 80C benefit (under old tax regime) would not be reversed. Moreover, the surrender value that you receive would be tax-free in your hands
under the provisions of Section 10(10D) if the premiums paid were up to 10% of the sum assured (for policies that have been issued on or after 1st April 2012) or 20% of the sum assured (for policies that were issued on or before 31st March 2012). Moreover, you can claim an exemption under the provisions of Section 10(10D) if the aggregate premiums paid are up to ₹2.5 lakhs for policies that have been issued on or after 1st February 2021. For policies that were issued before this date, there’s no maximum premium criterion, and the surrender value enjoys tax exemption if the premium is up to 10% or 20% of the sum assured, as the case may be.
How Policy Issue Date Affect Tax Rules?
As is evident from the previous section, the date when the policy was issued also affects the taxability of the surrender value. Here’s how -
1. ULIPs Issued Before 1st February 2021
If the ULIP was issued before 1st February 2021 and it is surrendered after the lock-in period of 5 years, the exemption under the provisions of Section 10(10D) would be available if the premium is up to 10% of the sum assured (20% for policies that were issued before 1st April 2012). 2. ULIPs Issued on 1st February 2021 or After this Date.
If you buy a ULIP anytime on or after 1st February 2021, the premium limit of ₹2.5 lakhs would apply. The surrender benefit would be tax-free only if the aggregate premium for all ULIPs is up to ₹2.5 lakhs and premium is up to 10% of the sum assured. If the premium is more than this amount, the Section 10(10D) exemption would not be allowed.
Key Takeaways
1. ULIPs are life insurance plans that provide life insurance protection along with the potential
of market-linked returns. 2. If you exit the ULIP before the maturity date, it is called surrendering the policy. 3. You are eligible for a surrender value, which can be taxed depending on when you surrender
the policy. 4. If you surrender the policy during the lock-in period of 5 years, the premium deduction
allowed under the provisions of Section 80C in the previous years would be reversed. 5. If you surrender the ULIP after the lock-in period, the surrender value would be tax-free
under the provisions of Section 10(10D) if the premium is up to 10% of the sum assured (for policies that have been issued on or after 1st April 2012) and the aggregate premium is up to ₹2.5 lakhs (for policies that were issued on or after 1st February 2021).
Conclusion
Surrendering a unit-linked insurance plan is not recommended, as you lose both the life insurance coverage and the potential for market-linked returns over the policy term. As such, it is feasible to continue ULIP till the chosen policy tenure. However, if you exit the ULIP prematurely and surrender it, you might earn a surrender value. Know the ULIP taxation on surrender, which depends on when
you surrender the policy, the premium to sum assured ratio, and the date you bought the ULIP. Knowing the tax implications of surrender can help you make informed financial decisions that can minimise your tax liability. You can also use a ULIP calculator to estimate the fund value that you can get on surrender or maturity.
FAQs
1. What is the meaning of surrendering in a ULIP policy?
Surrendering in a ULIP plan means exiting the policy before the maturity date. 2. What will happen if I choose to surrender my ULIP policy?
If you surrender the ULIP, you would get a surrender value. However, the treatment of the ULIP after surrender depends on when you surrender. If you surrender the policy within the lock-in period, the fund value after deducting the applicable discontinuance charges, shall be credited to the discontinued policy fund and the risk cover shall cease. At the end of the lock-in period, the proceeds of the discontinuance fund shall be paid to the policyholder. If the ULIPs were issued before 1st February 2021, would there be no surrender tax?
